title: EdgeRelations
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG.

  1. If $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ then we call $v_1$ and $v_2$ adjacent in $G$.
  2. Edges of the form $v_1 \hut v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_1$.
    Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_1 \huh v_2$ are called into $v_2$.
  3. Edges of the form $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$ are called out of $v_1$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg, and recall that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ and $L \subseteq V \times V$, with $L$ symmetric in its two coordinates.

  1. Adjacency. For $v_1, v_2 \in J \cup V$, the nodes $v_1$ and $v_2$ are called adjacent in $G$ iff \[ (v_1, v_2) \in E \;\lor\; (v_2, v_1) \in E \;\lor\; (v_1, v_2) \in L, \] equivalently iff $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ (def not-cdmg, item 7).
  2. Edge into a vertex. The relations into and out of below classify edges of $G$ (the underlying ordered pairs of $E$ or of $L$) relative to a single bound vertex parameter $v$ – not relative to a particular labelling $v_1, v_2$ of the edge's endpoints. For an ordered pair $e = (e_1, e_2) \in (J \cup V) \times (J \cup V)$ and a vertex $v \in J \cup V$, we call $e$ an edge into $v$ (in $G$) iff \[ \bigl(e \in E \;\land\; e_2 = v\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(e \in L \;\land\; (e_1 = v \,\lor\, e_2 = v)\bigr). \] In particular, only ordered pairs $e \in E \cup L$ can be edges into $v$.
  3. Edge out of a vertex. For an ordered pair $e = (e_1, e_2) \in (J \cup V) \times (J \cup V)$ and a vertex $v \in J \cup V$, we call $e$ an edge out of $v$ (in $G$) iff \[ e \in E \;\land\; e_1 = v. \] In particular, no $L$-edge is out of any vertex.
  4. Reconciliation with the source-block writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$, $v_1 \hut v_2$, $v_1 \huh v_2$. The two writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ both abbreviate the single underlying statement $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ (def not-cdmg, items 2–3); and the two writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ both abbreviate $(v_1, v_2) \in L$, which moreover coincides with $(v_2, v_1) \in L$ by the symmetry of $L$ (def def-cdmg). Hence membership of an edge in the categories into $v$ or out of $v$ depends only on the underlying element of $E$ or of $L$, not on which of these textual writings is used; in particular, the source block's listing $v_1 \tuh v_2$ or $v_2 \hut v_1$ in its item 3 and its single-writing listings in item 2 are equivalent under this identification.

    Spelled out per writing, items ii.–iii.\ above agree with the source block's items 2–3 as follows.

    • An $E$-edge written $v_1 \tuh v_2$ – i.e.\ underlying ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, equivalently written $v_2 \hut v_1$ – is into $v_2$ (clause ii.\ fires via $e \in E$ with $e_2 = v_2$) and out of $v_1$ (clause iii.\ fires via $e \in E$ with $e_1 = v_1$).
    • An $E$-edge written $v_1 \hut v_2$ – i.e.\ underlying ordered pair $(v_2, v_1) \in E$, equivalently written $v_2 \tuh v_1$ – is into $v_1$ and out of $v_2$, by the same clauses with $v_1$ and $v_2$ interchanged.
    • An $L$-edge written $v_1 \huh v_2$ – i.e.\ underlying ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$, equivalently $(v_2, v_1) \in L$ and equivalently written $v_2 \huh v_1$ – is into $v_1$ and into $v_2$ (the $L$-clause of ii.\ fires for either endpoint), and is out of neither $v_1$ nor $v_2$ (clause iii.\ requires $e \in E$, whereas $e \in L$).

    In particular, into $v$ and out of $v$ are not complementary classifications of edges incident at $v$: an $L$-edge incident at $v$ is into $v$ but never out of $v$.

lean_blocks.explanation: Section-level `variable` declaration that fixes the ambient node type `Node` for the rest of the file. The unbounded universe `Type*` lets `Node` live in any universe, matching the polymorphism style used throughout `CDMG.lean` and `CDMGNotation.lean`. The `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is required because `CDMG.E` and `CDMG.L` are `Finset (Node × Node)`, and Mathlib's `Finset` operations (membership, intersection, decidable equality lifted to pairs) need `DecidableEq` on the underlying carrier. Once declared with `variable`, every subsequent `def adjacent`, `def into`, `def outOf` in this file silently picks up `{Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments, so the signatures stay terse and uniform.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Polymorphic node carrier; `Type*` allows any universe.
-- `DecidableEq` is needed since `CDMG.E`, `CDMG.L` are `Finset (Node × Node)`.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code: def adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.sus v1 v2
lean_blocks.explanation: Adjacency is encoded as a one-line renaming of `G.sus v1 v2` (defined in `CDMGNotation.lean`). The notation block already unfolds `sus` to the three-disjunct $(v_1,v_2)\in E \lor (v_2,v_1)\in E \lor (v_1,v_2)\in L$, which is exactly item i of the LN. Re-spelling the disjunction here would duplicate `sus`'s body and force downstream proofs to juggle two names for one predicate; the renaming preserves the macro-grep correspondence with the LN. `def` (not `abbrev`) is chosen so that `adjacent` remains a named relation downstream — walks (`def_3_4`), family sets (`def_3_5`), and d-/$\sigma$-separation in chapters 6–7 all consume it as an abstraction; consumers needing the underlying disjunction can `unfold CDMG.adjacent` to land on `sus`. Symmetry is a free corollary via `hL_symm`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `adjacent` is a renaming of `G.sus`, which unfolds to `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`.
-- `def` (not `abbrev`) preserves the named abstraction downstream.
def adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.sus v1 v2

lean_blocks.code:
def into (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  (e ∈ G.E ∧ e.2 = v) ∨ (e ∈ G.L ∧ (e.1 = v ∨ e.2 = v))
lean_blocks.explanation: Predicate on `(v, e)`: an ordered pair `e : Node × Node` is *into* vertex `v` iff it is a directed edge with `v` at the head, or a bidirected edge with `v` at either endpoint. The two-clause split mirrors the LN literally: the `E`-clause checks `e.2 = v` (head position under the `tuh`/`hut` convention from `CDMGNotation.lean`); the `L`-clause checks `e.1 = v ∨ e.2 = v`, capturing both writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ of an $L$-edge — necessarily so, since `hL_symm` makes the two writings denote the same edge. Parameterising by a single bound `v : Node` (not an endpoint labelling) collapses the LN's asymmetric "into $v_1$ / into $v_2$" enumeration into one uniformly-quantified statement. No precondition `e ∈ E ∪ L` is threaded: any pair outside falls through both disjuncts and yields `False` automatically.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Edge-level predicate parameterised by a single bound vertex `v`.
-- Argument order `(v, e)`: `G.into v` partial-applies to a predicate on edges.
def into (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  -- `E`-clause: directed edge with `v` at the head (LN-faithful `e.2 = v`).
  -- `L`-clause: bidirected edge at either endpoint (both `huh` writings, via `hL_symm`).
  (e ∈ G.E ∧ e.2 = v) ∨ (e ∈ G.L ∧ (e.1 = v ∨ e.2 = v))

lean_blocks.code:
def outOf (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  e ∈ G.E ∧ e.1 = v
lean_blocks.explanation: Dual of `into`'s `E`-clause: an ordered pair `e` is *out of* `v` iff it is a directed edge (`e ∈ G.E`) with `v` at the tail (`e.1 = v`). Crucially, there is **no** `L`-clause — bidirected edges contribute nothing to `outOf`, by intentional design and per the LN. This is the second half of the `bidirected_huh_into_both_out_of_neither` subtlety: an $L$-edge incident at `v` is *into `v`* but *not out of `v`*, so `into` and `outOf` are **not** complementary classifications of edges incident at `v`. Downstream code that wants "incident but not into" must not reach for `outOf` as the complement. As with `into`, `v` ranges over the ambient `Node` rather than `G.J ∪ G.V`; `hE_subset` makes the predicate vacuously `False` for foreign vertices, so no membership obligation needs threading. Consumers include `def_3_5`'s children `Ch^G(v)` and `def_3_10`'s hard intervention edge removal.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `outOf v e`: only directed edges (`G.E`) with `v` at the tail.
-- Name uses Lean-idiomatic camelCase (`outOf`) since `out` is reserved-adjacent.
def outOf (G : CDMG Node) (v : Node) (e : Node × Node) : Prop :=
  -- Mirror of `into`'s `E`-clause: tail-at-`v` (`e.1 = v`) instead of head-at-`v`.
  -- No `L`-clause by design: bidirected edges are "out of neither" endpoint.
  e ∈ G.E ∧ e.1 = v

addition_to_the_LN: [inconsistent_writing_order_enumeration_into_vs_out_of] Throughout the enumeration above, the writings $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ denote the same edge (and the bidirected writings $v_1 \huh v_2$ and $v_2 \huh v_1$ likewise coincide). Membership of an edge in the categories "into $v$" or "out of $v$" depends only on the underlying element of $E$, not on which of these textual writings is used; item 3's explicit listing of both writings and item 2's single-writing enumeration are therefore equivalent under this identification.
design_choices:
**Encode `adjacent` as a one-line renaming of `sus`, not as a fresh three-disjunct.** The rewritten definition explicitly identifies adjacency with `v_1 \sus v_2 \in G`, and `sus` (from `CDMGNotation.lean`) already unfolds to the disjunction $(v_1,v_2) \in E \lor (v_2,v_1) \in E \lor (v_1,v_2) \in L$. Re-spelling that body inside `adjacent` was considered and rejected because it would duplicate `sus`'s implementation and force downstream proofs to carry two interchangeable names for the same predicate; the renaming preserves the macro-grep correspondence with the source notation.

**Use `def` rather than `abbrev` for all three predicates.** `adjacent`, `into`, and `outOf` are named relations the notes reuse as abstractions for walks, family sets, d-/$\sigma$-separation, and intervention. An `abbrev` would auto-unfold at every elaboration site, eliminating the named abstraction; downstream proofs that need the body can opt in via `unfold`.

**Make `into` and `outOf` predicates on ordered pairs $(v, e)$, not on labelled endpoint pairs $(v_1, v_2)$.** The source notes define "into $v_1$" and "into $v_2$" separately while defining only "out of $v_1$". Parameterising over a single bound vertex and a raw ordered edge collapses that asymmetry into one uniformly-quantified statement and resolves the wording subtlety where "out of $v_2$" was never literally defined. An alternative encoding indexed by endpoint pairs was rejected because it would propagate the source-text asymmetry into every consumer.

**Let `v` range over the ambient `Node`, not over `G.J \cup G.V`.** Restricting at the type level would force every downstream chain (walks, children, intervention) to thread a membership proof. Since `hE_subset` and `hL_subset` already guarantee edges sit inside $G$, supplying a foreign `v` makes the predicate vacuously `False`, so no side-condition is needed.

**Keep the `E`-clause and `L`-clause of `into` separate; reject a merged "incident at $v$" formula.** A unified condition like `v \in \{e.1, e.2\} \land (e \in E \lor e \in L)` would sweep up directed edges with $v$ at the tail, conflating "into" with "incident". Splitting on $E$ versus $L$ matches the literal reading and supports clean downstream case analysis.

**Omit any `L`-clause from `outOf` by design.** Bidirected edges are "into both endpoints" but "out of neither" — intrinsic to the notes' semantics of latent confounding. Adding an `L`-clause for symmetry with `into` was rejected as a deviation; the consequence (that `into` and `outOf` are *not* complementary classifications of edges incident at $v$) is flagged so downstream notions like out-degree or children never treat bidirected edges as "the remainder".

**Drop any precondition `e \in E \cup L` on the predicate arguments.** Threading membership through the type would burden every call site with an obligation the predicate body discharges anyway: any pair outside $E \cup L$ already falls through both disjuncts. The "in particular" clause of the notes is a consequence, not a hypothesis.
